1: The theme of covenant continuity: the names of Jacob's sons carried into Egypt are the seed of the nation that will be constituted at Sinai. Matthew's genealogy (Exodus 1:1-17) opens with the same narrative logic — names carried forward through darkness and exile, preserving the covenant line. The people of God exist before their deliverer; the deliverer is sent to a community already formed by divine promise.
2: The twelve sons of Jacob descend into Egypt; Jesus appoints twelve apostles to reconstitute Israel around himself (Matthew 10:1-4; Exodus 19:28). The number twelve is not incidental — it signals the reconstitution of all twelve tribes in the new covenant community. As the sons of Jacob were the seed of the nation, the twelve apostles are the foundation of the new Israel (Ephesians 2:20; Revelation 21:14).
3: Issachar and Zebulun — two of the tribes whose inheritance in Canaan will be in Galilee, the northern region. Matthew 4:13-16 quotes Isaiah 9:1-2: 'Land of Zebulun, land of Naphtali... the people living in darkness have seen a great light.' Jesus begins his ministry precisely in the territory of these two sons, fulfilling a prophecy rooted in this very tribal geography.
4: Benjamin, Dan, Naphtali, Gad, Asher — the sons of the concubines complete the twelve. The inclusion of sons from all four mothers in the covenant community anticipates the gathering of people from all nations into Christ (Galatians 3:28: 'There is neither Jew nor Gentile... you are all one in Christ Jesus'). The covenant community was always broader than a single bloodline.
5: Seventy persons descend into Egypt. Jesus sends out seventy-two (some manuscripts: seventy) disciples in Luke 10:1-24, paralleling the earlier mission of the twelve. As the seventy descendants of Jacob entered the land of bondage bearing the covenant promise, the seventy sent by Jesus enter towns bearing the kingdom of God. The correspondence between the two missions (and the joy of the commissioned on their return) reflects the same divine-commission pattern.
6: Joseph and all his brothers and that generation died — the mediating generation passes from the scene. The removal of the familiar mediator is the structural precondition for the suffering that calls forth the new, greater deliverer. Theologically: the covenant promise outlives every human mediator. Christ is the one mediator who does not die and leave the community exposed — he lives forever to intercede (Hebrews 7:25).
7: Israel's multiplication in the land of affliction — the more the people were afflicted, the more they grew (Exodus 1:12). The theme of life through death, growth through opposition, multiplication through persecution runs through redemptive history: the early church multiplied precisely as it was scattered under persecution (Acts 8:1-4). Jesus frames it as seed-logic: unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; if it dies, it bears much fruit (John 12:24). The covenant community cannot be suppressed by earthly power.
8: A new king who did not know Joseph — the rejection of the mediator, and the consequent persecution of those he protected. The pattern recurs in John 1:11: 'He came to his own, and his own did not receive him.' The world that benefited from Joseph's provision and Moses' liberation ultimately does not acknowledge the source of its blessing. The rejection of the mediator by the ruling power — Pharaoh ignoring Joseph, Pilate condemning Jesus — is a structural feature of redemptive history.
9: Pharaoh fears the covenant community — 'more numerous and stronger than we.' The earthly power perceives the people of God as a threat precisely because divine blessing cannot be contained within the world's categories of control. Revelation 12-13 depicts the same dynamic eschatologically: the dragon pursues the woman and her offspring because he cannot absorb or destroy what God is doing. The covenant people are always, in some sense, a threat to the kingdoms of this world.
10: Pharaoh's shrewdness deployed against the covenant people — the wisdom of this age confronting the divine purpose. Paul reflects on this pattern in 1 Corinthians 2:6-8: the rulers of this age did not understand the wisdom of God; if they had, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory. The very instrument by which the earthly powers tried to eliminate the threat (crucifixion) became the means of their defeat. Pharaoh's shrewd plan to stop Israel produced the exact outcome he feared; Pilate's verdict accomplished the exact purpose he sought to prevent.
11: Israel labors to build Egypt's store cities — the covenant people's labor under the world's authority. A shadow: the Servant of Isaiah 52-53 is 'despised and rejected,' 'oppressed and afflicted,' bearing burdens not his own. Jesus identifies himself with the laboring and heavy-laden (Matthew 11:28) precisely because the Son of God entered the condition of servitude to liberate those under it. The one who called Israel out of Egypt's service calls those under sin's burden to himself.
12: The more they were afflicted, the more they multiplied — the paradox of the indestructible covenant community. This principle governs the entire NT mission: persecution spreads the gospel (Acts 8:4); suffering produces endurance (Romans 5:3-4); death produces life (John 12:24). The blood of the martyrs is the seed of the church (Tertullian). Pharaoh's attempt to reduce Israel produces the numerical growth that makes his fear self-fulfilling — exactly as the authorities' attempt to suppress the early church produced its expansion.
13: Crushing labor (abodah parach) — the same term later forbidden in Israel's own slave law (Leviticus 25:43). A shadow: the Servant of Isaiah 53:4-5 carries grief and sorrows, is stricken and crushed (medakka) — the same crushing that characterizes Egypt's oppression is the suffering the Servant takes on for others. Christ takes on the crushing that Israel suffered from Egypt, and the crushing that all humanity suffers under sin, bearing it in himself.
14: Bitter labor in mortar and brick and all field service — the bitterness of Egypt, memorialized in the bitter herbs of the Passover feast. At Gethsemane, Jesus asks if the cup of bitterness can pass from him (Matthew 26:39) — the cup that Israel drank under Pharaoh becomes the cup the Servant drinks on behalf of all. The word Gethsemane means 'olive press' — the place of crushing. The bitterness of Egypt's service is contained within the bitterness of Gethsemane's prayer.
15: The midwives receive the death decree against Hebrew boys — the first of two death decrees in the Exodus narrative (the second in Exodus 1:22 extends to all Egypt). A type: the decree to kill male infants at birth is the exact form of Herod's response to news of the Messiah's birth in Bethlehem (Matthew 2:16). Matthew quotes Jeremiah 31:15 ('Rachel weeping for her children') to link the two events explicitly — the slaughter at Bethlehem is the Exodus pattern repeated in the fullness of time.
16: A type: the death decree against Hebrew boys by the earthly king parallels Herod's massacre of the innocents (Matthew 2:16-18) with structural precision: a powerful ruler fears the coming deliverer, issues a death decree against male infants, and the deliverer escapes while others die. Matthew explicitly connects the two events by citing Jeremiah 31:15. The decree intended to prevent the deliverer's emergence is the backdrop against which the deliverer's miraculous preservation is displayed.
17: The midwives feared God rather than the king — the first act of obedience to divine authority over human authority in redemptive history. The NT principle stated in Acts 5:29 ('We must obey God rather than human beings') has its founding moment here. The midwives who protected the infant Israelites under a death decree are the counterparts of those who protect and serve Jesus despite hostile authority — Joseph, who hid the family in Egypt; the magi, who did not report to Herod (Matthew 2:12).
18: Pharaoh interrogates the midwives about their disobedience — the covenant community called to account before earthly power for protecting what God is doing. Theme: the pattern recurs throughout the NT (Acts 4:7: 'By what power or what name did you do this?'). Those who stand before authority to answer for faithfulness to God participate in the same witness the midwives gave — calm, truthful, unafraid.
19: The midwives' reply — the Hebrew women are vigorous and give birth before they arrive. Theme: the life-force of the covenant community outpaces the reach of the death decree. The resurrection is the ultimate statement of this theme: the death decree of sin and law (Romans 6:23) is outrun by the life of God in Christ. Death cannot keep what Christ has made alive (Acts 2:24: 'it was impossible for death to keep its hold on him').
20: God was kind to the midwives and the people multiplied — divine blessing on faithful disobedience to a death decree. A revelation of God: YHWH rewards those who protect the vulnerable and honor him above earthly power. This divine character — rewarding those who align with God's purposes over against earthly authority — is fully expressed in Christ's beatitudes (Matthew 5:3-12: blessed are those who are persecuted for righteousness).
21: God gave the midwives families of their own — life for those who gave life. The principle of seed and harvest (Galatians 6:7-8) is illustrated in this reward: the midwives preserved the lives of Hebrew children; God gives them children. The theme of resurrection-reward — receiving back what was risked for God's sake — runs through the NT (Mark 10:29-30: 'no one who has left... for me and the gospel will fail to receive a hundred times as much').
22: A type: Pharaoh's edict to throw every Hebrew boy into the Nile directly parallels Herod's massacre of the innocents (Matthew 2:16-18). The NT does not make the connection allegorically — Matthew does it textually, citing Jeremiah 31:15 to show that both events are part of the same redemptive pattern. In both, an earthly king fears the deliverer and acts with mass violence; in both, the deliverer is preserved while the innocent die. The preserved deliverer carries the weight of the innocent who died for his sake.
